flashcards.front: Define the Selection operator (σ) in Relational Algebra. What is its purpose and syntax?
flashcards.back: Selection (σ) filters tuples from a relation based on a condition. Syntax: σ<condition>(R) returns all tuples where the condition evaluates to true. It is commutative (σ₁(σ₂(R)) = σ₂(σ₁(R))) and reduces the number of rows.

flashcards.front: Explain the Projection operator (π) in Relational Algebra. How does it differ from Selection?
flashcards.back: Projection (π) selects specific columns (attributes) from a relation, eliminating duplicate tuples. Syntax: π<attr1, attr2, ...>(R). Unlike Selection (which filters rows), Projection filters columns and may reduce cardinality; π_A,B(R) can produce fewer tuples than |R| due to duplicate elimination.

flashcards.front: What are the prerequisites for the Union operator (∪) to be applied on two relations? Is Union commutative?
flashcards.back: Two relations R and S must be union-compatible (same number of attributes with matching domains/typoes). Union returns all tuples from both relations, removing duplicates. Yes, Union is commutative: R ∪ S = S ∪ R. Example: π_city(Employee) ∪ π_city(Student) gives all distinct cities.

flashcards.front: Define Set Difference (−) in Relational Algebra with an example. Is it commutative?
flashcards.back: Set Difference R − S returns tuples that are in R but not in S. Both relations must be union-compatible. Example: Find students who are NOT enrolled in any course: Students − Enrolled. It is NOT commutative: R − S ≠ S − R in general.

flashcards.front: What is the Cartesian Product (×) operator? When is it used with Selection to form a theta join?
flashcards.back: Cartesian Product R × S combines every tuple of R with every tuple of S, producing |R| × |S| tuples. It is rarely used directly; when combined with Selection on common attributes, it forms a Theta Join: R ⋈_condition S = σ_condition(R × S). Example: σ_{Student.CourseID = Course.CourseID}(Student × Course).

flashcards.front: Explain the Rename operator (ρ). Why is it essential in complex Relational Algebra expressions?
flashcards.back: Rename (ρ) assigns new names to relations or their attributes to resolve naming conflicts. Syntax: ρ_{newName(attr1→new1, attr2→new2)}(R) or ρ_{newName}(R). Essential for Self-joins and when a relation is used multiple times in an expression, such as finding employees earning more than their managers: π_{E.Name}(σ_{E.Salary > M.Salary}(ρ_E(Emp) × ρ_M(Emp))).

flashcards.front: Define Natural Join (⋈) and explain how it differs from Cartesian Product followed by Selection on equi-attributes.
flashcards.back: Natural Join R ⋈ S combines relations on common attributes with equal values, projecting one copy of each common attribute. It is equivalent to: π_{all attributes}(σ_{R.A₁=S.A₁ ∧ ...}(R × S)). However, Natural Join automatically eliminates duplicate columns while R × S retains both; if no common attributes exist, Natural Join returns Cartesian Product.

flashcards.front: Explain the Division operator (÷) in Relational Algebra with an example. When is it typically used?
flashcards.back: Division R ÷ S returns tuples in R that relate to ALL tuples in S. Relations must satisfy: attributes of S must be a subset of attributes of R. Example: Find students enrolled in ALL courses: Enrollment ÷ π_{CourseID}(Courses). It is used for queries requiring 'for all' semantics; mathematically, R ÷ S = π_{R-S}(R) − π_{R-S}((π_{R-S}(R) × S) − R).
